title: Introduction
text: Dijkstra's algorithm is great for finding the optimal route, but what if you need more than just the best path? That's where Yen's algorithm comes in, offering you multiple ranked alternatives. This can be incredibly useful for risk management, uncovering underutilized paths, and comparing with historical operations. We'll start by practicing on the Movie graph and then apply what we've learned to logistics. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s662-000-new] As you can see here, the illustration compares Dijkstra's single optimal path with Yen's multiple ranked paths, highlighting the benefits of having several options.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to explain how Yen's algorithm finds multiple ranked paths between nodes. You'll learn how to configure the 'k' parameter to retrieve alternative routes and interpret path rankings for risk management. You'll also recognize when it's better to use Yen's algorithm over Dijkstra's, and vice versa.

title: What Yen's Does
text: Yen's algorithm is designed to find the k shortest paths between two nodes, not just the best route, but the best k routes, all ranked by total cost. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s664-000-existing] In this illustration, you can see multiple ranked paths from a source to a destination node, with paths colored from green, indicating the best, to yellow, indicating the fifth best.

title: Why Multiple Routes Matter
text: Having multiple routes is crucial for several reasons: capacity constraints might mean the optimal route is full, risk management requires backup options if disruptions occur, trade-off analysis allows you to compare time versus other factors, and historical comparison lets you see if current routes are in the top k. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s665-002-new] This diagram illustrates the importance of multiple routes in a network, highlighting scenarios like capacity constraints, risk management, and trade-off analysis.

title: How It Works
text: Yen's algorithm builds on Dijkstra's method. First, it finds the shortest path using Dijkstra's. Then, it systematically blocks edges to find the next-best paths. All discovered paths are ranked by total cost, and the top k are returned. This process guarantees that you find the k truly shortest paths. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s666-003-new] Here's a flowchart depicting the steps of Yen's algorithm, from finding the shortest path to ranking and returning the top k paths.

title: Yen's vs Dijkstra's
text: Let's compare Yen's and Dijkstra's algorithms. Dijkstra's returns a single best path, while Yen's provides the k best paths. If you're asking, 'What's the optimal route?' Dijkstra's is your go-to. But if you need options, Yen's is the answer. Internally, Dijkstra's makes a single pass, whereas Yen's runs Dijkstra's multiple times. Performance-wise, Dijkstra's is faster, but Yen's scales with k. Interestingly, for k equals 1, Yen's behaves exactly like Dijkstra's.

title: Configuration Parameters
text: When configuring Yen's algorithm, you'll need to set several parameters. The 'sourceNode' and 'targetNode' are integers representing the nodes you're interested in, and both are required. The 'k' parameter is an integer that specifies the number of shortest paths to compute, with a default of 1. You can also specify a 'relationshipWeightProperty' as a string, which is optional. Note that Yen's uses 'targetNode' singular, unlike Dijkstra's, which can handle multiple targets.

title: Project the Movie Graph
text: To project the Movie graph, we use the `gds.graph.project()` function with a Cypher projection. This involves matching actors who have collaborated on movies and counting those collaborations. The projected graph will then be used for pathfinding. Here's a Python snippet that demonstrates this process. After running it, you'll see the projected graph's name, along with the number of nodes and relationships it contains.

title: Find Multiple Paths
text: To find multiple paths, you'll call `gds.shortestPath.yens.stream()` with the `k` parameter. First, get the source and target node IDs. In this example, we're using Shah Rukh Khan and Lee Jung-jae. By running Yen's algorithm for the top 5 paths, you'll see not just the best path, but the top 4 alternatives as well.

title: Understanding the Results
text: The results from Yen's algorithm include several fields. The 'index' is an integer representing the 0-based rank of the path, with 0 being the best. 'TotalCost' is a float indicating the total cost from source to target. 'NodeIds' is a list of node IDs along the path, and 'costs' is a list of accumulated costs at each step. Finally, 'path' is a Cypher path entity for visualization. Paths are returned in order of total cost, with the lowest first.

title: Parallel Relationships
text: Yen's algorithm respects parallel relationships between nodes. If two actors collaborated on multiple movies, those are treated as separate edges in the projection. Yen's can find paths that use different parallel edges between the same nodes, which is particularly useful when these relationships have different weights. For instance, in a logistics network, the same route might cost more or less depending on the day. Including parallel relationships in your projection allows Yen's to choose the cheapest option. However, if you have a network with thousands of parallel edges and no weights, you'll always get the same path for your top k. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s672-009-new] This diagram illustrates how different edges between the same nodes can be used in pathfinding.

title: Weighted Paths
text: By adding collaboration counts as weights, you can find paths through the weakest collaborators. Remember, Yen's minimizes cost, so this approach finds paths through actors who collaborated the least. Here's a Python snippet showing how to run Yen's algorithm with collaboration counts as weights.

title: Yen's with inverted weights
text: Just like with Dijkstra's, you can invert the weights to find the strongest collaboration paths. You can project a new graph into the same session without disrupting the existing one. This involves calculating the inverse of collaboration counts, allowing you to find paths through the strongest collaborations. Here's how you can project a graph with inverted weights.

title: Run Yen's with inverted weights
text: Now, let's run Yen's algorithm with inverted weights to find the strongest collaboration paths. By using the inverted collaboration weights, you'll be able to identify paths that highlight the strongest connections between nodes.

title: Results
text: Here are the results of running Yen's algorithm with inverted weights. The paths are ranked by total cost, with the first path being the strongest collaboration path. For example, the path from Shah Rukh Khan through Deepika Padukone to Lee Jung-jae has a total cost of 2.4, indicating a strong collaboration path.

title: Interpreting results
text: In the previous version, when we minimized collaboration counts, every path had the same 4-hop cost because each hop had only one collaboration count. However, in the inverted version, Shah Rukh Khan has a high collaboration count and a shorter path through Deepika Padukone. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s677-014-new] This illustration compares the results of minimizing versus inverting collaboration counts, showing the impact on path selection.

title: Collaboration counts per hop
text: Here's a breakdown of collaboration counts per hop. From Shah Rukh Khan to Deepika Padukone, there are 5 collaborations. From Deepika Padukone to Donnie Yen, there's only 1. Donnie Yen to Simon Yam has 5, and Simon Yam to Lee Jung-jae has 1. This table helps you understand the collaboration dynamics at each step.

title: Complex operations, simple workflow
text: While Yen's algorithm makes it seem simple, the results demonstrate its ability to handle complex operations. Shah Rukh Khan has acted in 5 movies with Deepika Padukone, but Deepika has only acted in 1 movie with Donnie Yen. There are many other paths between Deepika and Lee Jung-jae with higher collaboration counts, but Yen's algorithm knows that using this low-count hop will get us to Lee Jung-jae faster than other paths with stronger collaborations. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s679-016-new] This diagram illustrates the complexity of paths in a graph and how Yen's algorithm navigates through various collaboration counts.

title: Performance Considerations
text: Yen's algorithm is more computationally expensive than Dijkstra's because it runs Dijkstra's internally for each of the k paths. The cost scales roughly with k, but it's still efficient for reasonable k values. Keep this in mind when deciding which algorithm to use.

title: When Yen's Won't Help
text: Yen's algorithm isn't the right choice in every situation. If you only need the single best path, Dijkstra's is more suitable. If you need paths from one source to multiple targets, use Dijkstra's with a targetNodes list. And if you need all shortest paths in the graph, consider using All Pairs Shortest Path instead.

title: Clean Up
text: Once you're done, it's important to clean up by dropping the projection and deleting the session. This ensures that resources are freed and billing is stopped. Here's a simple Python snippet to help you do just that.

title: From Movies to Logistics
text: You've now learned how to apply Yen's algorithm on the Movie graph, but the same principles apply to other contexts, like logistics. Multiple paths between actors can translate to alternative shipping routes. Paths ranked by hops or collaborations can be ranked by transit time. Comparing the top k paths can help you compare historical route choices. You've gained core skills in GDS, including projections, algorithms, Python workflows, and scalable analytics with Aura Graph Analytics. [IMAGE: img-4-aura-graph-analytics-3-yens-k-shortest-paths-s683-019-new] This illustration shows how Yen's algorithm can be transferred from movie graphs to logistics, highlighting key parallels and differences.

title: Summary
text: Yen's algorithm finds the k shortest paths between two nodes, enabling comparison and backup planning. Here's what we covered: it returns k paths ranked by total cost, builds on Dijkstra's by running it multiple times internally, and works with a single target only—unlike Dijkstra's, which can handle multiple targets. It respects parallel relationships, allowing paths using different edges between the same nodes. There are three execution modes: stream, mutate, and write. You've completed all the technical lessons in this workshop. Time to celebrate what you've accomplished!
